questions.question: What is a toxidrome?
options: A. A recognizable cluster of signs/symptoms associated with a category of toxic exposure
B. A specific antidote for a poison
C. A type of laboratory test
D. A rare, single-symptom presentation
questions.rationale: A toxidrome is a symptom cluster that lets providers act on a recognizable pattern without knowing the exact substance.

questions.question: What is the opioid toxidrome?
options: A. Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, altered mental status
B. Agitation, tachycardia, dilated pupils
C. Bradycardia with excessive secretions
D. Dry, flushed skin with urinary retention
questions.rationale: The opioid toxidrome is respiratory depression, pinpoint (miotic) pupils, and altered mental status.

questions.question: What is the sympathomimetic (stimulant) toxidrome?
options: A. Bradycardia and pinpoint pupils
B. Agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, dilated pupils
C. Excessive salivation and lacrimation
D. Dry skin and urinary retention alone
questions.rationale: Sympathomimetic toxidrome features agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, and dilated pupils.

questions.question: What findings characterize the anticholinergic toxidrome?
options: A. Altered mental status, dilated pupils, dry flushed skin, tachycardia, urinary retention
B. Excessive secretions and bradycardia
C. Pinpoint pupils and respiratory depression
D. Normal mental status with hypotension
questions.rationale: The anticholinergic toxidrome ('hot as a hare, dry as a bone, red as a beet, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter') includes altered mental status, dilated pupils, dry flushed skin, tachycardia, and urinary retention.

questions.question: What is the cholinergic toxidrome associated with?
options: A. Opioid medications
B. Organophosphate exposure
C. Antihistamine overdose
D. Cocaine use
questions.rationale: The cholinergic toxidrome (excessive secretions, bradycardia, muscle twitching) is classically associated with organophosphate exposure.

questions.question: What is the field priority order for suspected poisoning/overdose?
options: A. Identify the exact substance first, before any other action
B. Scene safety first, then support ABCs, identify substance/amount/time when possible, check glucose
C. Transport immediately with no on-scene assessment
D. Wait for poison control before taking any action
questions.rationale: Scene safety and ABC support take priority over exact substance identification, with glucose checked regardless of presumed cause.

questions.question: What distinguishes heat exhaustion from heat stroke?
options: A. Heat stroke involves altered mental status plus significantly elevated core temperature; heat exhaustion has normal-to-mildly-altered mental status
B. Heat exhaustion is always more severe than heat stroke
C. Heat stroke never involves elevated temperature
D. There is no meaningful clinical distinction
questions.rationale: Heat stroke is the life-threatening endpoint defined by significant altered mental status plus significantly elevated core temperature, distinguishing it from milder heat exhaustion.

questions.question: Does the presence of sweating rule out heat stroke?
options: A. Yes, heat stroke patients never sweat
B. No — some heat stroke patients still sweat, especially with exertional causes
C. Only in patients over 65
D. Sweating is irrelevant to heat illness diagnosis
questions.rationale: Sweating does not exclude heat stroke; altered mental status plus significantly elevated temperature is the defining combination regardless of sweating status.

questions.question: What is the appropriate field management for heat exhaustion (normal-to-mildly-altered mental status)?
options: A. Cooling, rest, and fluids (oral if tolerated and alert)
B. Aggressive ice-water immersion only
C. No treatment needed
D. Immediate advanced airway management
questions.rationale: Heat exhaustion is managed with cooling, rest, and fluids, distinguishing it from the aggressive cooling needed for heat stroke.

questions.question: What features characterize mild hypothermia?
options: A. Absent shivering and unresponsiveness
B. Shivering, mild confusion, intact coordination
C. Cardiac arrest
D. Severe muscle rigidity with no shivering
questions.rationale: Mild hypothermia presents with shivering, mild confusion, and intact coordination, distinguishing it from more severe stages.

questions.question: What features characterize severe hypothermia?
options: A. Vigorous shivering with clear mentation
B. Absent shivering, significantly altered/unresponsive mental status, risk of cardiac arrhythmia and arrest
C. Mild confusion only, with normal cardiac rhythm
D. Normal vital signs with intact coordination
questions.rationale: Severe hypothermia is marked by absent shivering, severe mental status changes, and significant cardiac risk.

questions.question: Why must a severely hypothermic patient be handled gently?
options: A. Rough movement can trigger a fatal arrhythmia in a severely hypothermic heart
B. Gentle handling has no physiologic basis and is purely a comfort measure
C. Rough handling only matters for trauma patients, not hypothermia
D. Hypothermic patients cannot develop arrhythmias
questions.rationale: A severely hypothermic heart is prone to arrhythmia, and rough movement/handling can precipitate a fatal rhythm disturbance.

questions.question: Should resuscitation be withheld from a hypothermic patient found in apparent cardiac arrest?
options: A. Yes, hypothermic arrest patients cannot be resuscitated
B. No — hypothermia can mimic death, and patients have survived prolonged downtime with good outcomes after rewarming; resuscitate per protocol
C. Only attempt resuscitation if the patient is under 40
D. Resuscitation should never be attempted in cold environments
questions.rationale: Hypothermia's protective, death-mimicking effect means resuscitation efforts should generally proceed per protocol rather than being presumed futile based on presentation alone.

questions.question: A patient found unresponsive with pinpoint pupils and a respiratory rate of 4/min near drug paraphernalia. What toxidrome does this match, and what is the priority?
options: A. Anticholinergic toxidrome; priority is IV fluids
B. Opioid toxidrome; priority is airway/ventilation support
C. Cholinergic toxidrome; priority is atropine only
D. Sympathomimetic toxidrome; priority is sedation
questions.rationale: Pinpoint pupils and severe respiratory depression match the opioid toxidrome, making airway/ventilation support the immediate priority.

questions.question: A hiker in hot weather has a temperature of 105°F, is confused, and is still visibly sweating. Should the sweating change the diagnosis of heat stroke?
options: A. Yes, sweating rules out heat stroke by definition
B. No — altered mental status plus significantly elevated temperature still meets the definition of heat stroke regardless of sweating
C. Sweating means this must be heat exhaustion instead
D. Sweating is not relevant to any heat illness assessment
questions.rationale: Sweating does not exclude heat stroke; the combination of altered mental status and significantly elevated core temperature defines heat stroke regardless of sweating status.
